title: Specifications for Accident Prevention Signs and Tags
issuing_body: Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), U.S. Department of Labor
scope: Safety signs, tags, and accident prevention markings used in general industry workplaces. Governs color coding, legend wording, signal word usage, and construction of accident prevention signage.
companion_standards: ANSI Z535.2 — Environmental and Facility Safety Signs
ANSI Z535.3 — Criteria for Safety Symbols
ANSI Z535.4 — Product Safety Signs and Labels
ANSI Z535.5 — Safety Tags and Barricade Tapes
ANSI Z535.6 — Product Safety Information in Product Manuals
note: OSHA 1910.145 was written before ANSI Z535 (1991). OSHA formally recognizes ANSI Z535 series as acceptable compliance method. For new installations ANSI Z535 is preferred; it is a superset of 1910.145 requirements.

sections.title: Scope and Application
requirements: 1910.145(a)(1): Applies to all accident prevention signs, tags, and similar devices used to prevent accidental injury or illness.
1910.145(a)(2): Does not apply to highway signs, fire-protection markings, or signals covered by other standards.
1910.145(a)(3): Signs must be used to prevent injury; safety signs are not a substitute for hazard elimination.

checklist.item: Confirm sign use is for accident prevention in a general industry workplace (not highway TCD).
Hazard has been evaluated — sign supplements, not replaces, hazard engineering controls.
checklist.notes: Hierarchy of controls: eliminate > substitute > engineer > admin > PPE > signage.

definitions.definition: A visual alerting device which gives warning of hazard or hazardous condition that may be life threatening.
Health hazard presenting risk of infection from organisms or their toxins.
A surface on which lettering or symbols are placed to convey a safety message.
A device usually made of card, paper, pasteboard, or plastic affixed to a hazardous item by string, wire, or adhesive.

subsections.title: Danger Signs
requirements: Used where an immediate hazard exists that WILL result in death or serious injury if not avoided.
Colors: RED background on header panel; WHITE lettering for signal word DANGER; BLACK body text on WHITE panel.
ANSI Z535 equivalent: Signal word DANGER, red signal word panel (Pantone 185C / RAL 3020).
No other design or color scheme shall be used for DANGER signs.

color_spec.header_background: #DA0000 (Safety Red per ANSI Z535.1 Color No. 11)

checklist.item: DANGER sign uses RED header panel with WHITE 'DANGER' signal word.
DANGER sign is used only for IMMEDIATE hazards (not for caution or general notice).
checklist.notes: Misuse of DANGER for non-immediate hazards is a common citation.

subsections.title: Caution Signs
requirements: Used to warn against potential hazards OR unsafe practices that may result in minor-to-moderate injury.
Colors: YELLOW background; BLACK lettering; BLACK upper panel with YELLOW CAUTION signal word.
ANSI Z535 equivalent: Signal word CAUTION, orange-yellow signal word panel.
Standard body: yellow background with black text; border black.
1910.145(c)(2)(ii): Yellow must be the predominant background color.

color_spec.header_background: #F5C800 (Safety Yellow per ANSI Z535.1 Color No. 69)

checklist.item: CAUTION sign uses YELLOW background (predominant color) with BLACK CAUTION signal word.
CAUTION used for hazards that MAY result in injury (not for immediate life threats — use DANGER).

subsections.title: Safety Instruction Signs
requirements: Used to provide general safety instructions and suggestions (not immediate or potential hazards).
Colors: WHITE background; GREEN panel with WHITE lettering for signal word; BLACK body text.
ANSI Z535 equivalent: NOTICE (blue) for informational, or SAFETY INSTRUCTIONS (green).
Example uses: Emergency exit routes, first aid locations, safety policy reminders.

color_spec.header_background: #007A00 (Safety Green per ANSI Z535.1 Color No. 71) or #0052A5 (Safety Blue for NOTICE)

checklist.item: Safety instruction sign uses GREEN header with WHITE text on WHITE body.

subsections.title: Slow-Moving Vehicle Emblem
requirements: Fluorescent orange triangle with dark red border — used on vehicles moving ≤ 25 mph.
Must comply with ASAE S276 standard dimensions.
Not applicable to static signs.

subsections.title: Biological Hazard Signs
requirements: Universal biohazard symbol must be used.
Colors: Fluorescent orange or orange-red background; BLACK biohazard symbol.
Must be used to identify equipment, containers, rooms, materials, or combinations thereof that contain or are contaminated with viable hazardous agents.

checklist.item: Biohazard symbol (ISO 7010 W009) used with fluorescent orange/orange-red background.

sections.title: Design Requirements — Signs
requirements: 1910.145(d)(1): Signs must be visible at all times when hazard exists; must be removed or covered when hazard no longer exists.
1910.145(d)(2): Wording must be concise, easy to understand, and accurately convey the message intended.
1910.145(d)(3): Illustrations and pictographs should supplement text where text alone is insufficient.
1910.145(d)(4): Signs must be legible from a safe viewing distance to warn of hazard before entering danger zone.
1910.145(d)(5): Letter height based on viewing distance — minimum per ANSI Z535.2 Table 1.
1910.145(d)(6): All signs must be free from sharp edges, burrs, or points that could cause injury during installation.
sections.note: Letter height table is per ANSI Z535.2 Table 1 (OSHA 1910.145(d)(5) references same proportions).

checklist.item: Sign is visible from the hazard approach point; not obscured by equipment or materials.
Sign wording is concise and uses positive action language (e.g., 'WEAR HARD HAT' not 'Hard hats must be worn').
Where workers may not read English: pictogram/symbol supplements text or replaces it.
checklist.notes: ISO 7010 safety symbols preferred for international comprehension.

checklist.item: Letter height is appropriate for the expected viewing distance (per ANSI Z535.2 Table 1 above).
Sign has no sharp edges or burrs on any accessible surfaces.

sections.title: Construction and Durability
requirements: 1910.145(e)(1): Signs must be made of material suitable for the environment of use.
1910.145(e)(2): Outdoor signs: UV-stable substrate (aluminum, galvanized steel, or rigid UV-stable plastic).
1910.145(e)(3): Indoor corrosive-environment signs: stainless steel, anodized aluminum, or coated steel.
1910.145(e)(4): Signs in wet or food processing areas: stainless steel or sealed substrate; no cardboard.
Minimum substrate gauge (aluminum): 0.040-in for rigid signs; 0.063-in for signs subject to wind loading.
Reflective sheeting: ASTM D4956 Type I minimum for indoor; Type III for outdoor/low-light areas.

substrate_guide.recommended_substrate: 0.080-in 5052-H32 aluminum with Kynar 500 coat or reflective
substrate_guide.finish: High-intensity prismatic sheeting (ASTM D4956 Type III)

checklist.item: Sign substrate is appropriate for the installation environment (see substrate guide).
Outdoor sign: UV-stable substrate and UV-stable print/laminate.
Wet-area sign: no cardboard, paper, or untreated wood substrate.
Sign fastening hardware is stainless steel or zinc-plated for corrosion resistance.

sections.title: Tags
requirements: 1910.145(f)(1): Tags must be used as a temporary means of warning of an existing hazard — they DO NOT replace safety signs.
1910.145(f)(2): Tags must be affixed as close as safely possible to the hazard they identify.
1910.145(f)(3): Tags must be readable and understandable by all employees who may encounter them.
1910.145(f)(4): Tags must be made of material (card, pasteboard, plastic) of minimum 14-mil (0.014-in) thickness.
1910.145(f)(5): Tag classification by color (same signal word scheme as signs):
DANGER: red/white/black; WARNING: orange/black; CAUTION: yellow/black; BIOHAZARD: fluorescent orange.
1910.145(f)(6): Tags must be attached with a non-reusable, self-locking device or equivalent.
1910.145(f)(7): Hazard identified on tag must be addressed before tag is removed; only authorized personnel may remove.

checklist.item: Lockout/tagout tags are temporary only — hazard must be corrected before tag removal.
Tag attached as close as safely possible to the hazardous point.
Tag material is min 14-mil thick card stock, pasteboard, or plastic.
Tag is attached with non-reusable, self-locking device (nylon zip-tie or equivalent).
Tag removal authorization: only trained and authorized employee may remove tag.
checklist.notes: OSHA 1910.147 (lockout/tagout) governs energy control procedures for equipment.

common_citation_patterns: 1910.145(c)(1): Using DANGER sign for non-immediate hazards (incorrect signal word).
1910.145(d)(4): Sign not visible from safe approach distance before entering hazard zone.
1910.145(d)(2): Wording is unclear, wordy, or in a language workers cannot read.
1910.145(e)(4): Paper or cardboard signs used in wet processing areas.
1910.145(f)(7): Tags left in place after hazard is corrected, or removed by unauthorized personnel.
install_notes: For new sign designs: use ANSI Z535 series — it is current, more detailed, and OSHA-accepted.
Verify sign visibility under all lighting conditions including emergency lighting only.
Include signs in facility Emergency Action Plan (EAP) map for evacuation routes.
Conduct annual sign condition inspection — replace faded, damaged, or illegible signs.
For bilingual workforces: provide signs in English and any language spoken by ≥10% of workers on site.
references: OSHA 29 CFR 1910.145 — Specifications for Accident Prevention Signs and Tags
OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 — Control of Hazardous Energy (Lockout/Tagout)
ANSI Z535.2-2023 — Environmental and Facility Safety Signs
ANSI Z535.3-2011 — Criteria for Safety Symbols
ANSI Z535.4-2023 — Product Safety Signs and Labels
ANSI Z535.5-2017 — Safety Tags and Barricade Tapes
ASTM D4956 — Standard Specification for Retroreflective Sheeting for Traffic Control
